title: How 3D Convolution Works
concept: 3D convolutions apply three-dimensional filters that slide across height, width, and time to capture spatial and temporal patterns simultaneously.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What dimensions does a spatiotemporal model analyze?
exercises.explain: Yes! Spatiotemporal models analyze the spatial dimensions (height and width) and the temporal dimension (time).

cards.title: From squares to cubes
cards.body: In 2D convolution, we slide a flat square filter across an image to find patterns like edges or corners. For video, we expand that filter into a solid cube! This cube slides not just left-to-right and up-and-down, but also forward-and-backward through time.

cards.title: 3D Convolution
cards.body: A 3D Convolution uses a filter kernel with three dimensions: $k_t imes k_h imes k_w$ (temporal depth, height, width). It performs element-wise multiplications across a 3D block of pixels, outputting a single number representing a local spatiotemporal feature.

cards.title: A sliding scanner cube
cards.body: Imagine a clear glass cube with patterns printed inside it. To scan a book, you slide a magnifying glass over a flat page. To scan a video stack, you push this glass cube through the pile of pages, looking at multiple pages at once as you slide it.

cards.title: A 3x3x3 filter
cards.body: A typical 3D convolutional kernel has a size of 3x3x3. This means the filter looks at a 3x3 pixel area across 3 consecutive frames at the same time to calculate a single output value.

cards.title: Different from 2D with channels
cards.body: Don't confuse 2D convolution on multi-channel images with 3D convolution! In a 2D convolution, the filter covers the entire channel depth and slides in 2 directions. In 3D convolution, the filter is shorter than the frame depth and slides in **3 directions** (height, width, and frames).

cards.title: 2D vs. 3D Convolutions
cards.body: Visualizing how the filters slide through data:

left.text:
Filter: Height x Width
Slides: 2 directions (X, Y)
Output: 2D feature map

right.text:
Filter: Time x Height x Width
Slides: 3 directions (X, Y, Time)
Output: 3D feature map

exercises.prompt: In what directions does a 3D convolution filter slide?
exercises.explain: Correct! A 3D convolution slides across height, width, and time frames.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A 3D convolution filter slides across color channels in the same way it slides across frames.
exercises.explain: False. Convolutions typically integrate color channels completely, while sliding across spatial and temporal dimensions.

exercises.sentence: A 3D convolutional kernel with size 3x5x5 processes 3 ___ and a 5x5 spatial area.
exercises.explain: The first number in the spatiotemporal kernel dimensions (3x5x5) represents the temporal window size, measured in frames.

exercises.prompt: Which filter dimensions represent a 3D convolutional kernel?
exercises.explain: A 3D kernel has three dimensions, representing temporal depth, height, and width (e.g., 3x3x3).

exercises.prompt: If you apply a 3D convolution with a temporal kernel size of 5 to a 16-frame video without padding, how many temporal steps will the output feature map have (assuming a temporal stride of 1)?
exercises.explain: Exactly! Calculated using the 1D convolution arithmetic: $Output = Input - Kernel + 1 = 16 - 5 + 1 = 12$ temporal steps. Next: let's explore how skipping frames affects this calculation!
